records.ety: [L. assimilatus, p. p. of assimilare; ad + similare to make like, similis like. See Similar, Assemble, Assimilate.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Assimilated (<?/); p. pr. & vb. n. Assimilating (<?/).]

defs: To bring to a likeness or to conformity; to cause a resemblance between.
quotes: To assimilate our law to the law of Scotland.
Fast falls a fleecy; the downy flakes Assimilate all objects.

defs: To appropriate and transform or incorporate into the substance of the assimilating body; to absorb or appropriate, as nourishment; as, food is assimilated and converted into organic tissue.
quotes: Hence also animals and vegetables may assimilate their nourishment.
His mind had no power to assimilate the lessons.

defs: To become similar or like something else.
To change and appropriate nourishment so as to make it a part of the substance of the assimilating body.
quotes: Aliment easily assimilated or turned into blood.

defs: To be converted into the substance of the assimilating body; to become incorporated; as, some kinds of food assimilate more readily than others.
quotes: I am a foreign material, and cannot assimilate with the church of England.
